channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: Space jam: astronomers detect ‘raspberry sugar’ on dust cloud in Milky Way - The Guardian
item.description:
1. Space jam: astronomers detect ‘raspberry sugar’ on dust cloud in Milky Way  The Guardian
  2. Detection of a four-carbon sugar in interstellar space  Nature
  3. Key type of sugar found near center of Milky Way galaxy  Yahoo
  4. A Sweet Surprise: Scientists Find Sugar Deep in Our Galaxy  The New York Times
  5. In a sweet discovery, astronomers find sugar lurking in the space between stars  ABC News - Breaking News, Latest News and Videos

  5. FCC Approves Reflect Orbital Demo Satellite to Test Redirecting Sunlight  Via Satellite

item.title: Why is Venus hotter than Mercury, when Mercury is closer to the Sun? - The Conversation
item.description:
1. Why is Venus hotter than Mercury, when Mercury is closer to the Sun?  The Conversation
  2. Amid the Stars: Earth’s Evil Twin  mountain-news.com
  3. About 50 kilometres above Venus’ surface, the crushing heat and pressure below give way to temperatures and air pressure surprisingly close to Earth’s, a region NASA has described as the most Earth-surface-like environment in the solar system — at least b  Space Daily
  4. What would happen to a human being on the surface of Venus?  Universe Space Tech

item.title: Around 252 million years ago, volcanoes across what is now Siberia erupted repeatedly for more than a million years, releasing perhaps 100,000 billion tonnes of carbon dioxide and helping wipe out roughly 90 per cent of marine species in the deadliest mas - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Around 252 million years ago, volcanoes across what is now Siberia erupted repeatedly for more than a million years, releasing perhaps 100,000 billion tonnes of carbon dioxide and helping wipe out roughly 90 per cent of marine species in the deadliest mas  Space Daily
  2. Researchers confirm cause of Earth’s biggest mass extinction  Stanford Doerr School of Sustainability
  3. Scientists finally solved the mystery of Earth's greatest mass extinction  ScienceDaily
  4. Metabolic vulnerability confirmed as cause of Earth’s biggest mass extinction  Open Access Government
  5. Scientists detect Earth's deadliest extinction signs in today's oceans: Great Dying started with warming s  The Economic Times

item.title: A solar eclipse is coming to Europe this summer - The Washington Post
item.description:
1. A solar eclipse is coming to Europe this summer  The Washington Post
  2. A total solar eclipse is coming in August. Will you be able to see it?  USA Today
  3. This summer's sunset solar eclipse will be like nothing you've ever seen. It's a rare, beautiful sight you won't want to miss  BBC Sky at Night Magazine
  4. 10 best Spanish cities to see the total solar eclipse 2026  Space
  5. 1 month until the total solar eclipse 2026 — Here's what you need to know  Space

item.title: Oldest quasars ever discovered add to 'perplexing' space mystery - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Oldest quasars ever discovered add to 'perplexing' space mystery  Phys.org
  2. Euclid Telescope Finds Quasars Within 700 Million Years of the Big Bang  Sky & Telescope
  3. Ancient quasars add to a 'major unsolved problem' in astrophysics  Reuters
  4. Scientists have discovered the oldest quasar ever seen, and it shines with the light of a trillion suns  Space
  5. In July 2026, Europe's Euclid telescope found 31 of the oldest quasars ever seen, two now shining with the light of a trillion suns when the cosmos was just 5 percent of its present age — their glow 13 billion years in the coming  Space Daily

item.title: ‘Disappointed’: Cochrane journal asked researchers to publish article, then retracted it for conflicts - Retraction Watch
item.description: ‘Disappointed’: Cochrane journal asked researchers to publish article, then retracted it for conflicts  Retraction Watch

item.title: Spacecraft Heading for Asteroid NASA Slapped so Hard It Changed Its Orbit Gets Crucial OTA Update - autoevolution
item.description:
1. Spacecraft Heading for Asteroid NASA Slapped so Hard It Changed Its Orbit Gets Crucial OTA Update  autoevolution
  2. A spacecraft called DART was deliberately crashed into an asteroid in 2022 to test whether humanity could deflect one, and it worked — the asteroid's orbit changed measurably, the first time humans have intentionally altered the movement of a natural body in  ScienceBlog.com
  3. NASA mission changed orbit of asteroid around Sun  The Grainger College of Engineering
  4. What Hera Will Find At The Asteroid DART Crashed Into  WorldAtlas

  3. James Webb telescope captures never-before-seen glimpse of 'Centaur' galaxy's battle wounds — Space photo of the week  Live Science
  4. James Webb Space Telescope celebrates its 4th birthday with stunning image of a galaxy crash site  Space
  5. Galaxy Mergers Aren't Always Obvious  Universe Today

item.description:
1. Gravitational waves reveal hidden populations within black hole mergers  Phys.org

  3. Evidence Mounts for Hierarchical Black Hole Mergers  American Physical Society
  4. Scientists Say Some Black Holes Are Born From Other Black Holes  Gizmodo
  5. Many black holes may not be born the way we thought  Tech Explorist

item.description:
1. Chinese Spacecraft Approaches Mysterious Object Near Earth  Yahoo News New Zealand

  3. China releases 1st photo of Earth's elusive 'quasi-moon' Kamo'oalewa  Space
  4. In Deep Space, China Meets Earth’s ‘Amazing’ Quasi-Moon  The New York Times
  5. Secretive Chinese probe snaps first photo of Earth's mysterious 'quasi-moon' — and it may pose a big problem  Live Science

item.title: Physicists recreate black hole energy extraction in the lab - ScienceDaily
item.description:
1. Physicists recreate black hole energy extraction in the lab  ScienceDaily
  2. Synthetic rotation brings black hole energy theory into lab, amplifying waves  Phys.org
  3. Physicists Replicate Black Hole Energy Extraction Using Synthetic Rotation  Tech Times
  4. CUNY ASRC Recreates Extreme Black Hole Physics in the Lab  동아사이언스
  5. CUNY physicists recreate black hole energy extraction in a historic lab experiment  The Brighter Side of News

item.title: Voyager's signal now reaches Earth as a whisper measured in fractions of a billionth of a watt, fainter than almost anything our antennas were built to hear - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Voyager's signal now reaches Earth as a whisper measured in fractions of a billionth of a watt, fainter than almost anything our antennas were built to hear  Space Daily
  2. Voyager 1 Has Traveled Through Space at 38,000 Mph Since 1977 And Still Hasn't Reached 1 Light-Day  ScienceAlert
  3. One Light-Day Away: Voyager's Epic Voyage  Rediff
  4. Voyager Spacecraft: The Ultimate Power Management Challenge?  EE Times
  5. Voyager 1 was launched in 1977 with a suite of 10 science instruments — as of 2026, only two remain switched on, and the spacecraft's nuclear power source loses roughly 4 watts every year, a slow drain that is now deciding what humanity still gets to learn fr  ScienceBlog.com

item.title: Tiny waves in the deep ocean can affect the climate thousands of kilometres away - The Conversation
item.description:
1. Tiny waves in the deep ocean can affect the climate thousands of kilometres away  The Conversation
  2. The "butterfly effect" of the ocean floor: what is it & how does it affect climate change? Cambridge scientists explain  yourweather.co.uk
  3. Climatic reach of small-scale turbulence in the ocean interior  Nature
  4. Hidden deep-sea turbulence could alter climate and fisheries within one lifetime  Phys.org

item.title: When Italian and German researchers modeled a square-meter array of 1,482 neodymium magnets, the simulation showed roughly a fifth of incoming low-energy solar protons being deflected — suggesting a potential way for deep-space crews to reduce som - Space Daily
item.description:
1. When Italian and German researchers modeled a square-meter array of 1,482 neodymium magnets, the simulation showed roughly a fifth of incoming low-energy solar protons being deflected — suggesting a potential way for deep-space crews to reduce som  Space Daily
  2. Could Permanent Magnets Protect Astronauts from Solar Storms?  Universe Today
  3. Can permanent magnets protect astronauts from solar storms?  Universe Space Tech

item.title: Birds are not descended from dinosaurs — they are dinosaurs, a living branch of the theropod family that survived the asteroid and now fills the world with more than 10,000 species - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Birds are not descended from dinosaurs — they are dinosaurs, a living branch of the theropod family that survived the asteroid and now fills the world with more than 10,000 species  Space Daily
  2. Letter: The joy of living alongside Earth's last dinosaurs  New Scientist
  3. From T. rex to turkeys: Steve Brusatte on ‘The Story of Birds’  WGN Radio 720

item.description: Firefly brightness holds a cautionary tale about accepting older measurements  Phys.org

item.title: Quantum-gravitational mechanism could explain the universe's homogeneity - Phys.org
item.description: Quantum-gravitational mechanism could explain the universe's homogeneity  Phys.org

item.title: The rivers on Titan flow, pool into lakes and are fed by rainfall on a slow cycle much like our own, but the liquid is methane and the ground beneath it is water frozen hard as stone - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The rivers on Titan flow, pool into lakes and are fed by rainfall on a slow cycle much like our own, but the liquid is methane and the ground beneath it is water frozen hard as stone  Space Daily
  2. Titan has rivers, rainfall, lakes and a slow hydrological cycle exactly like Earth's, except that every drop of it is liquid methane and the rock the rivers run over is water ice  Space Daily

item.title: The most common type of planet discovered in the Milky Way may be a world that does not exist in our solar system — larger than Earth, smaller than Neptune, and wrapped in an atmosphere we barely understand - Space Daily
item.description: The most common type of planet discovered in the Milky Way may be a world that does not exist in our solar system — larger than Earth, smaller than Neptune, and wrapped in an atmosphere we barely understand  Space Daily

item.title: Self‑building molecular rings bring next‑generation drug delivery and smart materials closer - Phys.org
item.description: Self‑building molecular rings bring next‑generation drug delivery and smart materials closer  Phys.org

item.title: Earth’s inner core is not rock but a sphere of solid iron-rich metal, smaller than the Moon yet probably more massive. As the planet gradually loses heat, liquid metal freezes onto its surface, expanding it by roughly a millimetre each year. - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Earth’s inner core is not rock but a sphere of solid iron-rich metal, smaller than the Moon yet probably more massive. As the planet gradually loses heat, liquid metal freezes onto its surface, expanding it by roughly a millimetre each year.  Space Daily
  2. Academia Sinica - Research Findings | Earth’s mantle locally heats up our planet’s core  sinica.edu.tw
  3. Earth’s inner core is an iron-rich ball as hot as the surface of the Sun, kept solid only because the crushing pressure of the entire planet raises the temperature at which iron can melt  Space Daily

item.title: Light from the Sun takes about eight minutes to reach Earth, but the energy inside a single photon can spend tens of thousands of years bouncing through the Sun's dense core before it ever escapes the surface to begin that eight-minute trip - Space Daily
item.description: Light from the Sun takes about eight minutes to reach Earth, but the energy inside a single photon can spend tens of thousands of years bouncing through the Sun's dense core before it ever escapes the surface to begin that eight-minute trip  Space Daily

item.title: Space junk debris cloud discovered in high-traffic orbit 'is a potential minefield' for the costliest satellites - Space
item.description:
1. Space junk debris cloud discovered in high-traffic orbit 'is a potential minefield' for the costliest satellites  Space
  2. Falling spacecraft debris increasingly surviving reentry, raising safety concerns  MSN
  3. Tiny 2-inch space debris discovered in key satellite orbit, scientists warn  The News International
  4. Astronomers are finding space junk too small to track, and it's getting dangerous  Yahoo
  5. Adapting Satellite Design to the Growing Space Debris Problem  AZoQuantum

item.title: Europa, one of Jupiter’s moons, is sealed beneath a shell of ice, but the ocean hidden below may contain twice as much liquid water as every ocean on Earth combined — and Jupiter’s gravity may have kept it liquid for billions of years - Space Daily
item.description: Europa, one of Jupiter’s moons, is sealed beneath a shell of ice, but the ocean hidden below may contain twice as much liquid water as every ocean on Earth combined — and Jupiter’s gravity may have kept it liquid for billions of years  Space Daily

item.title: A single teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about four billion tonnes on Earth — roughly the mass of a mountain — because the star has crushed the equivalent of the Sun into a sphere the width of a city - Space Daily
item.description:
1. A single teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about four billion tonnes on Earth — roughly the mass of a mountain — because the star has crushed the equivalent of the Sun into a sphere the width of a city  Space Daily
  2. A neutron star can pack more mass than our entire Sun into a sphere only about 20 kilometres across — roughly the size of a city — and a teaspoon of its material would weigh about a billion tonnes on Earth, more than every human being alive combined.  Space Daily

item.title: Rescue robot of tomorrow may be a cockroach in scuba suit - Fox News
item.description:
1. Rescue robot of tomorrow may be a cockroach in scuba suit  Fox News
  2. Scientists build tiny 'diving suit' for cockroaches, turning them into search-and-rescue cyborgs  Live Science
  3. Japan lab steers 'insect cyborgs' toward disaster search missions  毎日新聞
  4. Cockroach in a Wetsuit: 4-Inch Cyborg Insect Navigates Flooded Disaster Zones  finance.biggo.com
  5. Cyborg Cockroach Gets 3D-Printed Diving Suit: Flood Rescue Breakthrough Using Biomimetic Insects  Pandaily

item.title: A rare meteorite examined in 2026 revealed evidence of a Moon-sized world that orbited the young Sun more than 4.5 billion years ago, then vanished — possibly shattered in a catastrophic collision while Earth was still being assembled - Space Daily
item.description: A rare meteorite examined in 2026 revealed evidence of a Moon-sized world that orbited the young Sun more than 4.5 billion years ago, then vanished — possibly shattered in a catastrophic collision while Earth was still being assembled  Space Daily

  3. Fig. 1 (IMAGE)  EurekAlert!
  4. 💧 Water would be made of two liquids in one  Techno-Science.net
  5. Machine learning evaluation of structural descriptors for supercooled water  Nature

item.title: Leafcutter ants don't eat the leaves they carry home in long green columns — they feed the clippings to an underground fungus, and it is the fungus they farm and eat, a practice that predates human agriculture by around 50 million years - Space Daily
item.description: Leafcutter ants don't eat the leaves they carry home in long green columns — they feed the clippings to an underground fungus, and it is the fungus they farm and eat, a practice that predates human agriculture by around 50 million years  Space Daily

item.title: If you compressed the entire history of Earth into a single calendar year, humans appear at 11:36 pm on the 31st of December, agriculture at 11:59, and everything written down in history occupies the final 15 seconds before midnight - Space Daily
item.description: If you compressed the entire history of Earth into a single calendar year, humans appear at 11:36 pm on the 31st of December, agriculture at 11:59, and everything written down in history occupies the final 15 seconds before midnight  Space Daily

1. Vantablack: World's Blackest Paint Could Solve A Major Problem For Astronomy  ScienceAlert

  3. Vantablack, the world's blackest paint, is shown to have the potential to prevent light pollution from satellites.  GIGAZINE
  4. Ultra-Black Coating Reduces Satellite Glare  Optics & Photonics News
